spec_match.rationale: Live demo runs `new RegExp(input)` vs `new RegExp(RegExp.escape(input))` against an editable haystack, showing the spec's headline behaviour: metacharacters get neutralised so a string-as-literal pattern matches verbatim.

interactive.rationale: Real input + textarea controls drive a live update that builds two regexes per keystroke and renders the match list, with a feature-detect probe at the top — well past the static-card bar.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Feature index says `the long-missing escape function for safely building regexes from user input`; the page is exactly that — one user input, one safe-vs-naive comparison.

edge_cases.rationale: Naive-vs-safe is shown for common metacharacters, but the spec's leading-digit hex-escape rule (`\xHH` for a leading `0-9` so the pattern still matches after a `\0` / `\1`) is never demonstrated, and TypeError on non-string input is also not surfaced.

references.rationale: `see also` cites https://chromestatus.com/feature/5179154657705984 and the TC39 proposal.

design_system.rationale: Inline styles use CSS variables only, no raw hex, and there are no button-style overrides that could create light-on-light contrast bugs.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the leading-digit hex-escape rule
openQuestions.detail: The spec specifically hex-escapes a leading ASCII digit/letter so the result remains a valid pattern after a preceding `\0` or `\1` decimal escape. Add a row to the comparison that pipes `1abc` and `0test` through `RegExp.escape` and shows the resulting `\x31abc` / `\x30test` output — this is the most subtle thing the spec does and the demo silently glosses over it.

openQuestions.title: Show the TypeError path for non-string input
openQuestions.detail: `RegExp.escape(123)` is required to throw TypeError per spec, not coerce. Add a small `try it with a number/null/undefined` button so visitors see the failure mode explicitly — right now the demo only feeds strings.

openQuestions.title: Surface the raw escaped output as a copyable line
openQuestions.detail: Both panels show the matches but the actual escaped string is only labelled `used pattern`. Adding a one-click copy button next to it would make the page a more useful day-to-day reference and reinforce that what `RegExp.escape` returns is just a string.

summary: Strong, honest demo of the headline `escape user input before compiling a regex` pattern with a real feature-detect probe and a polyfill fallback. Missing only the spec's subtler edge cases — leading-digit hex escape and the TypeError-on-non-string requirement — both of which are easy adds.
